Amplitude (AMPL) Stock Is Up, What You Need To Know
data.description:
What Happened?

Shares of digital analytics platform Amplitude jumped 3% in the morning session after the company outlined its strategic initiatives, including AI integration and financial targets, at Citi's 2025 Global TMT Conference. 

CFO Andrew Casey highlighted the company's evolution from a digital tool to a comprehensive platform integrating AI and analytics. Investors reacted positively to key financial metrics shared, notably a 31% increase in total Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO) last quarter, which indicates strong future revenue visibility. The company also detailed plans to improve margins by reducing sales and marketing expenses from 44% of revenue to the mid-30s. The stock's rise was also supported by a broader positive sentiment in the technology sector, as the US Tech stock index showed signs of resuming growth ahead of new labor market data.

After the initial pop the shares cooled down to $11.34, up 4.2% from previous close.

Is now the time to buy Amplitude? Access our full analysis report here, it’s free.

What Is The Market Telling Us

Amplitude’s shares are very volatile and have had 29 moves greater than 5% over the last year. In that context, today’s move indicates the market considers this news meaningful but not something that would fundamentally change its perception of the business.

The previous big move we wrote about was 3 days ago when the stock dropped 4.4% on the news that the major indices continued to retreat amid profit-taking and renewed concerns about tariffs. 

Investors reacted to a federal court ruling that most of President Trump's global tariffs were illegal, raising uncertainty over trade policy and the fiscal impact of potential refunds. Rising Treasury yields added to the pressure, with the 10-year climbing above 4.2% and the 30-year nearing 5%, intensifying worries about stretched equity valuations. September's historically weak track record for stocks further dampened sentiment, leaving traders cautious ahead of the jobs report later in the week and the Federal Reserve's upcoming rate decision.

Amplitude is up 5.9% since the beginning of the year, but at $11.34 per share, it is still trading 21.5% below its 52-week high of $14.44 from February 2025. Investors who bought $1,000 worth of Amplitude’s shares at the IPO in September 2021 would now be looking at an investment worth $206.84.

Here at StockStory, we certainly understand the potential of thematic investing. Diverse winners from Microsoft (MSFT) to Alphabet (GOOG), Coca-Cola (KO) to Monster Beverage (MNST) could all have been identified as promising growth stories with a megatrend driving the growth. So, in that spirit, we’ve identified a relatively under-the-radar profitable growth stock benefiting from the rise of AI, available to you FREE via this link.

data.title: Why Sirius XM (SIRI) Stock Is Trading Up Today
data.description:
What Happened?

Shares of satellite radio and media company Sirius XM jumped 2% in the morning session after company executives expressed confidence in renewing its contract with star host Howard Stern, dispelling recent rumors about his potential departure. 

The comments, made at the Bank of America 2025 Media, Communications & Entertainment Conference, addressed speculation that Stern had been fired, as his current deal is set to expire at the end of 2025. 

SiriusXM's CEO, Jennifer Witz, stated, “I’m confident we’ll get to the right place,” emphasizing that Stern has been “core to our platform for over 20 years.” Adding to the positive sentiment, the company also announced it is teaming up with entertainment company IGN to produce a new radio show focused on gaming.

After the initial pop the shares cooled down to $24.00, up 1.8% from previous close.

Is now the time to buy Sirius XM? Access our full analysis report here, it’s free.

What Is The Market Telling Us

Sirius XM’s shares are very volatile and have had 21 moves greater than 5% over the last year. In that context, today’s move indicates the market considers this news meaningful but not something that would fundamentally change its perception of the business.

The previous big move we wrote about was 16 days ago when the stock dropped 3.7% as the major indices continued to pull back, with technology stocks accounting for most of the market's largest decliners. 

A key reason for this trend is that much of the recent market gains were concentrated in the "AI trade," which includes these large technology and semiconductor companies. So this could also mean that some investors are locking in some gains ahead of more definitive feedback from the Fed. 

Despite the downturn, some analysts viewed this as an opportunity to own some of the "Core AI winners." Dan Ives of Wedbush Securities commented, "In our view, the tech bull cycle will be well intact for at least another 2-3 years, given the trillions being spent on AI infrastructure/software/chips/power/apps looking ahead. This remains our tech playbook and investor roadmap." 

Additionally, mixed earnings reports from retailers, such as Target, added to the market's weakness. 

Sirius XM is up 8.6% since the beginning of the year, but at $24.00 per share, it is still trading 17.5% below its 52-week high of $29.10 from September 2024. Investors who bought $1,000 worth of Sirius XM’s shares 5 years ago would now be looking at an investment worth $426.97.

Today’s young investors won’t have read the timeless lessons in Gorilla Game: Picking Winners In High Technology because it was written more than 20 years ago when Microsoft and Apple were first establishing their supremacy. But if we apply the same principles, then enterprise software stocks leveraging their own generative AI capabilities may well be the Gorillas of the future. So, in that spirit, we are excited to present our Special Free Report on a profitable, fast-growing enterprise software stock that is already riding the automation wave and looking to catch the generative AI next.

data.title: Google hit with $3.45 billion EU antitrust fine over adtech practices
data.description:
Alphabet's Google was hit with a 2.95-billion-euro ($3.45 billion) EU antitrust fine on Friday for anti-competitive practices in its lucrative adtech business, a sharp sanction against the firm that could rile up U.S. President Donald Trump.

The fine, the fourth penalty Google has faced in its decade-long fight with EU competition regulators, comes amid bubbling trade tensions between major global powers and U.S. threats of retaliation over EU scrutiny of American tech firms.

The move by the European Commission was triggered by a complaint from the European Publishers Council. Trump, who has hit Europe with trade tariffs, has threatened to retaliate against the European Union for any push back against Big Tech.

While Google plans to appeal, the Commission has warned of stronger remedies - including potential divestitures - if the company fails to address its conflicts of interest. The case underscores growing transatlantic friction over digital market regulation and the EU's push to rein in dominant platforms.

The EU competition enforcer had originally planned to hand out the fine on Monday but opposition from EU trade chief Maros Sefcovic on concerns about the impact on U.S. tariffs on European cars derailed EU antitrust chief Teresa Ribera's plan.

The Commission said Google favoured its own online display technology services that reinforced its own ad exchange AdX's central role in the adtech supply chain and allowed Google to charge high fees for its service, to the detriment of rivals and online publishers.

Google abused its market power since 2014 until today, the EU watchdog said.

It ordered Google to stop the self-preferencing practices and take measures to cease its inherent conflicts of interest. The company has 60 days to inform the Commission how it plans to comply with this order, and another 30 days to do so.

The Commission reiterated its preliminary view that Google should divest part of its services but said it wants to first hear and assess Google's compliance efforts, confirming a Reuters story last year.

"Google must now come forward with a serious remedy to address its conflicts of interest, and if it fails to do so, we will not hesitate to impose strong remedies," Ribera said in a statement.

"Digital markets exist to serve people and must be grounded in trust and fairness. And when markets fail, public institutions must act to prevent dominant players from abusing their power," she said.

'WE WILL APPEAL'

Google criticised the EU decision and said it would challenge it in court.

"The European Commission's decision about our ad tech services is wrong and we will appeal. It imposes an unjustified fine and requires changes that will hurt thousands of European businesses by making it harder for them to make money," Lee-Anne Mulholland, Vice President, Global Head of Regulatory Affairs, said in a statement.

"There's nothing anticompetitive in providing services for ad buyers and sellers, and there are more alternatives to our services than ever before."

The latest fine compared with a record 4.3 billion euro penalty handed out to Google in 2018, 2.42 billion euros in 2017 and a 1.49 billion euros in 2019.

Reuters reported last week that the fine would be modest, marking a change in Ribera's approach with her predecessor's deterrent hefty fines.

The European Publishers Council lamented the absence of a breakup order.

"A fine will not fix Google's abuse of its adtech," its executive director Angela Mills Wade said.

"Without strong and decisive enforcement, Google will simply write this off as a cost of business while consolidating its dominance in the AI era, perpetuating unfair competition and weakening news media and publishing companies which rely on advertising revenues," she said.

Cori Crider, Senior Fellow at Future of Tech Institute, and an Honorary Professor at UCL Laws, urged the Commission to take a drastic step with a breakup order.

"Europe made an important stand for the rule of law today by pressing ahead with this first-step fine in the face of Trump and Big Tech's bullying," she said.

"But I want to be clear: only a break-up will fix Google’s monopoly, unlock this €120bn market for European business, and save our dying media sector."

Google is scheduled to go to trial in the United States on September 22 to determine remedies in a separate case brought by the U.S. Justice Department where a judge found the company holds illegal monopolies in online advertising technology.

Google's 2024 advertising revenue, including from search services, Gmail, Google Play, Google Maps, YouTube, Google Ad Manager, AdMob and AdSense, amounted to $264.6 billion or 75.6% of total revenue. It is the world's dominant digital-advertising platform.

Google does not provide revenue figures for its adtech business which relates to advertising on other websites and not search ads.

($1 = 0.8542 euros)

data.title: 1 800 FLOWERS COM INC SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
1-800-FLOWERS.COM, Inc., a leading provider of floral and gourmet food gifts, has released its Form 10-K report for fiscal 2025. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

Total Net Revenues: $1,685.7 million, a decrease of 8.0% compared to fiscal 2024, primarily due to continued slowing demand for everyday gifting occasions as discretionary income remains under pressure.

Gross Profit: $652.3 million, a decrease of 11.2% from fiscal 2024, with a gross margin of 38.7%, down 140 basis points due to higher cost of merchandise and deleveraging of fixed costs.

Net Loss: $200.0 million, compared with a net loss of $6.1 million in fiscal 2024, driven by lower sales, reduced gross margin, and increased advertising costs.

Basic and Diluted Net Loss Per Common Share: $(3.13), compared to $(0.09) in fiscal 2024, reflecting the significant increase in net loss.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The company operates in three business segments: Consumer Floral & Gifts, Gourmet Foods & Gift Baskets, and BloomNet. Each segment includes a variety of brands and product offerings, such as 1-800-Flowers.com, Personalization Mall, Harry & David, and BloomNet services.

New Product Launches: The company expanded its product offerings with the acquisition of Scharffen Berger, a manufacturer of premium chocolate and specialty treats, and Card Isle, an e-commerce greeting card company, enhancing its presence in the greeting card category.

Operational Strategy: The company is focused on building a 'Celebratory Ecosystem' by integrating its premium gifting brands and expanding its suite of products and services to enhance customer relationships and increase purchase frequency.

Future Outlook: For fiscal 2026, the company aims to transform into a customer-centric, data-driven organization with a focus on driving cost savings, organizational efficiency, and broadening its reach into new channels.

Acquisitions: Recent acquisitions include Things Remembered, Card Isle, and Scharffen Berger, which are expected to enhance the company's product offerings and market presence.

Operational Challenges: The company faced challenges due to macroeconomic conditions, including a decline in consumer confidence and a highly promotional environment during the holidays, impacting demand for everyday gifting occasions.

Technology and Infrastructure: The company continues to invest in technology infrastructure to enhance the customer shopping experience and improve operational efficiency, including the use of advanced analytics and marketing automation technologies.

Seasonality: The company's business is highly seasonal, with significant revenue generated during the Thanksgiving through Christmas holiday season, as well as during major floral gifting occasions like Mother’s Day and Valentine’s Day.

Strategic Initiatives

Strategic Initiatives: The company is focusing on transforming into a customer-centric, data-driven organization to support its multi-year Celebrations strategy and fuel future growth. Strategic priorities include driving cost savings and organizational efficiency, broadening reach beyond e-commerce into new channels, and strengthening the team through enhanced talent and accountability.

Capital Management: The company repurchased $10.2 million worth of its own shares during fiscal 2025 under its stock repurchase plan, with $11.4 million remaining authorized for future repurchases. The company made payments of $30.0 million on its Term Loan, including a $25.0 million voluntary prepayment, and had no amounts outstanding under the revolving credit facility as of June 29, 2025. The company does not anticipate paying cash dividends in the foreseeable future due to existing credit facility limitations.

Future Outlook: For fiscal 2026, the company expects to borrow under its revolving credit facility to fund pre-holiday manufacturing and inventory purchases, with plans to repay all working capital borrowings by the end of the second quarter. The company aims to position itself for sustainable revenue and profit growth through strategic initiatives focused on agility and customer-centricity.

Challenges and Risks

Market Competition: The company faces significant competition across its product categories, including floral, plant, gift basket, and gourmet foods. This competition could lead to price reductions, decreased revenues, and lower profit margins.

Supply Chain Risks: The company is exposed to risks related to its reliance on international vendors for flowers and other products, which could be affected by regulatory changes, political instability, and trade restrictions.

Macroeconomic Conditions: The company is subject to macroeconomic conditions that could impact consumer spending, such as high unemployment, fluctuating interest rates, and economic uncertainty. These factors could reduce demand for the company's products.

Operational Risks: The company faces risks from increased shipping costs and supply chain disruptions, which could affect product delivery and customer satisfaction.

Cybersecurity Threats: The company is exposed to cybersecurity threats that could harm its reputation and result in significant costs.

Regulatory Risks: Compliance with evolving laws related to e-commerce, privacy, and data protection could increase operational costs and affect business practices.

Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks, including fluctuations in foreign currency exchange rates, which could impact financial results. Additionally, changes in interest rates could affect the company's borrowing costs and financial performance. The company is also subject to risks related to commodity price fluctuations, which could impact the cost of raw materials and overall profitability.

SEC Filing: 1 800 FLOWERS COM INC [ FLWS ] - 10-K - Sep. 05, 2025

data.title: Toronto Stocks Inch Higher, Enghouse Systems Fall on 3Q Profit, Revenue Declines
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Toronto-listed stocks edged forward.

Sector performance was mixed Friday. Primary losses in energy, tech services, and commercial services offset gains in materials, tech and health services stocks.

Canada's S&P/TSX Composite Index rose by 0.2% to 28972.10 and the blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 was virtually unchanged at 1715.55.

On the employment front, Canada's unemployment rate rose more than expected in August, hitting 7.1%, its highest since May 2016 outside of the height of the pandemic. According to Statistics Canada, the economy lost a net 65,500 jobs last month, on top of 40,800 lost the month before. Market expectations were for employment to rise by 10,000.

Shares in Enghouse Systems fell by 8.8% to 20.82 Canadian dollars ($15.06) after reporting lower profit and revenue in the fiscal third quarter as the technology company grapples with macroeconomic uncertainties.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: FTSE 100 closes lower, dragged down by energy, banks; investors assess data
data.description:
Key points:
  • FTSE 100 down 0.1%, FTSE 250 up 0.5%
  • Entain rises after Jefferies upgrade
  • Women's soccer, hot weather boost UK retail sales in July
  • US job growth nearly stalls in August

Britain's FTSE 100 closed lower on Friday, dragged down by energy and bank stocks, while investors assessed domestic and U.S. economic data.

The blue-chip FTSE 100 CURRENCYCOM:UK100 was 0.1% lower on the day but ended the week marginally higher.

The domestically focused FTSE 250 closed 0.5% higher but logged its second straight weekly decline.

In the market, the homebuilders' index (.FTNMX402020) rose, led by Berkeley , up 3% after reaffirming its profit forecast for fiscal years 2026 and 2027.

Peers Vistry , Persimmon , Taylor Wimpey and Barratt Redrow also advanced.

Precious metal miners and industrial miners (.FTNMX551020) rose, tracking higher gold and copper prices, respectively.

Conversely, energy stocks fell 2.4% and weighed on the FTSE 100, with giants Shell and BP down 2.2% and 2.6%, respectively.

Heavyweight bank stocks (.FTNMX301010) fell and top lenders HSBC , NatWest , Barclays , and Lloyds were among the biggest laggards on the benchmark index.

Non-life insurers (.FTNMX303020) fell, dragged by Admiral Group's (ADM.L) 2.9% decline, top loser on FTSE 100, after Peel Hunt downgraded the stock to "sell" from "reduce".

In other moves, Entain rose 3.3%, to top the FTSE 100, after Jefferies raised price target on the betting company.

Ashmore fell 4.3% after the asset manager reported lower-than-expected fee revenue and a dip in profit in its annual results.

Concerns over Britain's finances and the government's ability to keep them under control weighed on the markets earlier this week, briefly sending yields on long-dated government bonds to a 27-year high.

Investors continue to speculate about tax rises that could dampen economic growth, with Britain set to deliver its budget on November 26.

On the data front, retail sales rose more than expected in July.

In the U.S., data showed job growth weakened sharply in August and the unemployment rate rose to 4.3%, confirming labour market conditions were softening and sealing the case for an interest-rate cut from the Federal Reserve this month.

British Deputy Prime Minister Angela Rayner resigned after saying she deeply regretted her mistake of underpaying property tax on a new home.

data.title: Tesla Board Proposes Musk Pay Package Worth as Much as $1 Trillion Over Decade — WSJ
data.description:
By Gareth Vipers and Theo Francis

Tesla's board is asking investors to approve a new pay package for Chief Executive Elon Musk that could deliver to him as much as $1 trillion in stock over the next decade.

The proposed arrangements could see Musk, already the world's wealthiest individual, awarded installments of shares if Tesla hits a series of market-capitalization and operating or financial milestones, according to a securities filing published Friday.

The maximum payout would give him an additional 12% stake in Tesla, if the company reaches a market capitalization of $8.5 trillion — nearly eight times its current valuation. At that market value, the proposed award would be valued at slightly more than $1 trillion, or about the same as the company's entire current market capitalization.

Tesla's board said the unprecedented pay package was aimed at keeping Musk focused on the electric carmaker. The Tesla brand and its sales have taken a hit from Musk's political activities and now-frayed alliance with President Trump. This spring, Tesla board members reached out to executive search firms to work on a formal process for finding Tesla's next CEO and some pressed the longtime leader to spend more time at the company, The Wall Street Journal previously reported.

Tesla Chair Robyn Denholm later said the board had confidence in Musk and its growth plan, and Musk stepped aside from his White House advisory role in May.

"Retaining and incentivizing Elon is fundamental to Tesla...becoming the most valuable company in history," Denholm said in a letter to investors Friday. The package was "designed to align extraordinary long-term shareholder value with incentives that will drive peak performance from our visionary leader."

The proposal would lift Musk's stake in the electric-vehicle maker to as much as 29% if all of the targets are met, according to the filing, also boosting his voting power. Musk has previously said he wanted roughly 25% voting control of Tesla to keep his focus on the company.

Tesla shares were up about 3% Friday morning.

20 million Teslas

In addition to market-cap milestones, payouts would depend on Tesla hitting any of a dozen business and financial targets, though not in any particular order. These include delivering 20 million Tesla vehicles or a million non-vehicle robots over the next decade. Tesla has sold about eight million vehicles.

Other product goals include putting a million robotaxis into service and reaching a three-month average of 10 million subscribers for its Full Self Driving service. A single robotaxi could also count as a delivered vehicle and a Full Self Driving subscription.

Eight tranches could be earned based on reaching adjusted profit thresholds ranging from $50 billion to $400 billion. The measure reflects earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization, and excludes the effects of asset impairments, stock-based pay and gains and losses on digital assets such as cryptocurrency. Tesla reported $16.6 billion in adjusted Ebitda for 2024.

To illustrate the scale of the market-cap targets, the company described each as a multiple of another company's current market valuation: The first was described as 36 times General Motors, while others were described as double Apple or Nvidia's values.

The filing also includes a shareholder proposal for Tesla to invest in Musk's artificial-intelligence startup, xAI. Musk has repeatedly mobilized his business empire to boost xAI, with SpaceX agreeing in July to invest $2 billion in it. Tesla's board didn't offer a recommendation on how investors should vote.

Musk floated the idea in July, writing on his social-media platform X: "If it was up to me, Tesla would have invested in xAI long ago."

Musk's commitment questioned

In recent years, some Tesla investors started to question the CEO's commitment to the company, as Musk dedicated more of his time and energy to political causes. In 2022, he completed a $44 billion takeover of Twitter. Musk, a self-described "free speech absolutist," spent an increasing amount of time posting on the platform.

Musk later cozied up to Trump, donating vast sums to his election campaign and eventually acting as a close White House adviser. That relationship eventually soured.

Despite Musk's evangelism about the promise of Tesla's growth, the business has been in a funk this year amid falling vehicle sales. Tesla's profit dropped 71% in the first quarter and 16% in the second.

Shareholders are due to vote on the proposals on Nov. 6 . The new pay package requires approval by a majority of shares voted, including those held by Musk and his brother Kimbal Musk, also a Tesla director. Some prior pay votes at Tesla excluded their shares.

Tesla didn't immediately respond to requests for comment Friday.

Unusual provisions

Musk's new pay proposal is complex: As the company achieves each market-capitalization target, a 12th of the underlying 424 million shares would be earned if Tesla also meets new adjusted profit or operating targets. These earned shares, however, would only vest — or become fully Musk's property — at two key moments, roughly in spring 2032 and fall 2035 .

Moreover, Musk is required to hold shares for at least five years from the time they are earned, even if they have already vested. Typically, recipients may sell shares once they vest.

The proxy notes that Musk would receive voting rights to the shares awarded under the new pay package once market-cap and other targets are achieved, potentially before they are vested and he gains full ownership of them. Until then, the shares will be voted proportionally to those of other shareholders, the proxy says.

In some circumstances, Musk could earn some tranches of shares without meeting product goals, in the event of a natural disaster, war, pandemic or new laws or government actions that restrict the design, performance, sale or marketing of a product.

An unusual provision in the award could essentially wipe out the value of shares he has earned if Tesla's share price falls far enough by the time the shares vest — to $334 for shares earned before 2030, or the share price in spring 2032 for any earned subsequently.

The scale of Musk's compensation at Tesla has been a point of contention in the past. His 2018 pay deal was struck down by a Delaware judge, who said the process was deeply flawed and criticized the company's board for a lack of transparency.

Tesla's directors said the then-record stock-option deal, which amounted to more than $55 billion in compensation, was necessary to keep Musk focused on the carmaker amid a slump in sales and increased competition from overseas. He has run the company without a pay package since then, though last month the Tesla board approved an "interim" stock award for Musk that it tentatively valued at $23.7 billion . The interim award depends on the outcome of an ongoing appeal over his 2018 pay deal.

Write to Gareth Vipers at gareth.vipers@wsj.com and Theo Francis at theo.francis@wsj.com

data.title: K Wave Media Rises After Unit Chosen as Fund General Manager
data.description:
By Chris Wack

Shares of K Wave Media climbed after its Solaire Partners subsidiary was selected to manage a fund jointly financed by South Korea's three major IPTV operators.

K Wave Media's stock was 12% higher, at $2.50, midday Friday, but has fallen about 79% since the start of the year.

The entertainment and bitcoin treasury company said Friday that Solaire Partners was chosen to manage the 40 billion won ($28.7 million) fund financed by KT, SK Broadband, and LG Uplus.

The fund formation represents a strategic initiative to revitalize the IPTV Video on Demand market, K Wave Media said.

The company said the focus of the Solaire IPTV Video Investment Fund is to secure high-quality content optimized for IPTV platforms while accelerating the delivery of the latest releases to viewers.

Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com

Purepoint Uranium Closes Final Tranche of $6 Million Private Placement
data.description:
Toronto, Ontario--(Newsfile Corp. - September 5, 2025) - Purepoint Uranium Group Inc. (OTCQB: PTUUF) ("Purepoint" or the "Company") announces the closing of the final tranche of its previously announced private placement (the "Private Placement") comprising of a combination of:

  • 5,768,824 Saskatchewan charity flow through units (the "SK Flow Through Units") at a price of $0.65 per unit for aggregate gross proceeds of $3,749,735.60; and
  • 3,041,295 National charity flow through units (the "NT Flow Through Units", together with the SK Flow Through Units, the "Flow Through Units") at a price of $0.59 per unit for aggregate gross proceeds of $1,794,364.05.

"This final tranche not only completes our raise but strengthens our alignment with IsoEnergy and reinforces our shared commitment to long-term uranium discovery in the Basin," said Chris Frostad, President & CEO of Purepoint. "With exploration now underway across several properties, this financing ensures we can move into the fall and winter seasons with both momentum and flexibility."

Each Flow-Through Unit consists of one common share in the capital of the Company to be issued on a "flow through" basis pursuant to the Income Tax Act (Canada) and one common share purchase warrant ("Warrant"). Each Warrant entitles its holder to purchase one common share in the capital of the Company at an exercise price of $0.50 per share for a period of 24 months from the date of issue. Together with the first tranche of the Private Placement that closed on August 29, 2025, the Company has issued a total of 772,946 traditional flow through units, 5,768,824 SK Flow Through Units and 3,041,295 NT Flow Through Units for aggregate gross proceeds of $6,000,137.79.

In connection with the closing of the final tranche of the Private Placement, the Company paid Ventum Financial Corp., Stephen Avenue Securities Inc., and Canaccord Genuity Corp. finders' fees consisting of, in aggregate, $106,662.14 in cash and 264,111 non-transferable compensation warrants. Each compensation warrant entitles its holder to purchase one common share in the capital of the Company at an exercise price of $0.50 per share for a period of 24 months from the closing date.

The proceeds of the Private Placement will be used for the exploration and advancement of the Company's projects in the Athabasca Basin, Saskatchewan. All securities issued in connection with the closing of the final tranche of the Private Placement are subject to a four-month hold period pursuant to the applicable securities laws with an expiry date of January 6, 2026. The closing is subject to final acceptance by TSX Venture Exchange of the Private Placement.

In connection with the Private Placement, IsoEnergy Ltd. (OTCQX: ISENF) ("IsoEnergy") acquired 2,531,646 SK Flow Through Units. Acquisition of the SK Flow Through Units by IsoEnergy is considered a "related party transaction" pursuant to Multilateral Instrument 61-101 - Protection of Minority Security Holders in Special Transactions ("MI 61-101"). IsoEnergy is considered a related party of the Company under MI 61-101 by virtue of holding 10.6% of the issued and outstanding common shares of the Company on a non-diluted basis prior to its participation in the Private Placement. The Company was exempt from the requirements to obtain a formal valuation or minority shareholder approval in connection with IsoEnergy's participation in the Private Placement in reliance of sections 5.5(a) and 5.7(1)(a) of MI 61-101. A material change report will be filed in connection with the participation of IsoEnergy in the Private Placement less than 21 days in advance of the closing of the Private Placement, which the Company deemed reasonable in the circumstances so as to be able to avail itself of potential financing opportunities and complete the Private Placement in an expeditious manner.

Following completion of the Private Placement, IsoEnergy owns an aggregate of 9,864,980 Common Shares and 5,864,980 Warrants, representing approximately 12.57% of Purepoint's issued and outstanding Common Shares on a non-diluted basis, and approximately 18.65% of Purepoint's issued and outstanding Common Shares on a partially diluted basis, assuming full exercise of the Warrants held by IsoEnergy. While IsoEnergy currently has no plans or intentions with respect to the Purepoint securities, IsoEnergy may develop such plans or intentions in the future and, at such time, may from time to time acquire additional securities, dispose of some or all of the existing or additional securities or may continue to hold the Common Shares, Warrants or other securities of Purepoint based on market conditions, general economic and industry conditions, trading prices of Purepoint's securities, Purepoint's business, financial condition and prospects and/or other relevant factors. A copy of the early warning report filed by IsoEnergy will be available under Purepoint's profile on SEDAR+ at www.sedarplus.ca or by contacting Graham du Preez, Chief Financial Officer of IsoEnergy, at 306-373-6399. IsoEnergy's head office is located at 217 Queen St. West, Suite 401, Toronto, Ontario, M5V 0R2.

About Purepoint

Purepoint Uranium Group Inc. (OTCQB: PTUUF) is a focused explorer with a dynamic portfolio of advanced projects within the renowned Athabasca Basin in Canada. Highly prospective uranium projects are actively operated on behalf of partnerships with industry leaders including Cameco Corporation, Orano Canada Inc. and IsoEnergy Ltd.

Additionally, the Company holds a promising VHMS project currently optioned to and strategically positioned adjacent to and on trend with Foran Corporation's McIlvena Bay project. Through a robust and proactive exploration strategy, Purepoint is solidifying its position as a leading explorer in one of the globe's most significant uranium districts.

data.title: Lululemon Faces Further Pain Ahead as Casual Offerings Grow Stale — Analysis
data.description:
(16:37 GMT) *Lululemon Could Face Further Guidance Cuts Ahead, Analysts Say
(16:37 GMT) *Lululemon Shares Likely to Remain Under Pressure Amid Product Struggles in U.S., Analysts Say
(16:37 GMT) Lululemon Faces Further Pain Ahead as Casual Offerings Grow Stale — Analysis

By Kelly Cloonan

Some of Lululemon's core products aren't generating the same buzz they used to in an increasingly crowded market for casual athleticwear, a dynamic that could keep the company's performance under pressure for some time.

The brand known for its yoga pants and scuba sweatshirts spooked investors on Thursday with a cut to sales and profit guidance for the year after second-quarter results fell short of expectations, with underperformance in its U.S. business and a bigger hit from tariffs. Shares dropped 19% Friday and are down about 56% this year.

Chief Executive Calvin McDonald offered a stark critique of some of the problems behind the company's recent subpar results, calling its loungewear offerings "stale" and citing intensifying competition in recent years.

Some analysts noted that Lululemon's tone has shifted, with executives more overtly acknowledging the competitive threats to its perch in the activewear dominion as its efforts to spur demand with new colors fell flat. While the company proposed solutions, including ramping up new product introductions, the challenges will be hard to overcome quickly and will likely weigh on shares for some time, especially if its annual outlook is cut further as some expect.

"This print highlighted many long-term concerns around the stock," Morgan Stanley analyst Alex Straton said in a research note, pointing to intensifying competition and saturation in the U.S. market, among other risks. She suggested that Lululemon shares now deserve a valuation similar to a specialty apparel retailer, rather than its previous status as a high-growth apparel brand.

Jefferies analyst Randal Konik said that the company's second consecutive cut to its annual outlook shows it has lost visibility into its business as it contends with volatile sales. It recorded its weakest month of the quarter in July, when most retailers saw their strongest sales, and its lackluster third-quarter outlook suggests a tough back-to-school shopping period, he said.

"When you see something like that, it means that while the brand's relevant, it's becoming less relevant," Konik said, noting that Lululemon was the "it" brand during the Covid-19 pandemic. Its casual offerings have since lost ground to new entrants like Alo Yoga and Vuori, he said.

Konik forecasts steeper guidance cuts through the end of the year and sales declines in 2026, noting the brand seems to be losing business to competitors at a faster clip.

Much of next year's performance will hinge on whether the company can spark demand with new casual styles, William Blair analyst Sharon Zackfia said, acknowledging its performance apparel continues to draw shoppers.

"Forty percent of the mix is non-performance apparel, so they need to figure out a way to get that into a better, firmer footing so that it's not dragging down the overall business," she said.

Signs of weakness in its once-strong China business and a bigger-than-expected tariff headwind only add further pressure, she said.

"All of those dynamics really create a lot less profitability in the near term, and probably a greater lack of clarity on how quickly trends rebound and when," she said. "I think that's kind of what the Street is really grappling with today."

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

The electronic-signature company recently signed an agreement with the General Services Administration to give government agencies a discount on Docusign products, Thygesen says. Docusign's work with federal agencies has been relatively small, but it presents a large opportunity, he says. The company is also trying to push more AI tools beyond just signatures, which help summarize and analyze agreements, and is aiming for larger customers. Shares up 5.3%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1214 ET - Secondhand shopping has surged this year with consumers turning to thrift stores as tariffs cause price hikes in conventional apparel. But thrift stores were gaining even before tariffs, according to a report from Placer.ai. Visits to thrift stores, which rose 7.9% year-over-year in the second quarter, have risen 39.5% from the second quarter of 2019, while the broader clothing industry has seen only 9.5% growth over that span. Economic pressures and sustainability concerns have fueled that growth, which has extended beyond thrift store's traditional base to include more visits from rural and suburban consumers and higher-income demographics, the report says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1155 ET - Toll Brothers is dialing back some of its home starts to clear its accumulated inventory of spec houses in a dormant housing market, says Wedbush analyst Jay McCanless. Toll Brothers primarily builds higher-end, luxury homes and as a result may not be suffering from the competitive supply that is forcing builders to offer aggressive incentives in the entry-level homes market. Still, McCanless says Toll Brothers management is hoping to have two or fewer spec homes per community, which "should prevent any inventory buildup." Management is aiming for a mix of 40% affordable luxury homes, 40% move up/luxury and 20% active adult, McCanless says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1128 ET - Toll Brothers is seeing less of a sales slowdown than other homebuilders amid high mortgage rates and affordability concerns because it caters to a wealthier buyer with its average home price over $1 million. As a result, it's not as dependent on mortgage rate buy downs as other companies. But it still needs to offer some incentives to complete sales especially in its affordable luxury segment, which may continue to hurt its margins in the near-term, says Wedbush analyst Jay McCanless. "Toll is still offering design center and other incentives along with selected buy downs to spur traffic," McCanless says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1108 ET - Cracker Barrel has reverted to its old logo after pressure from customers and the White House — but that doesn't answer the question of where its million-dollar marketing strategy will head now, Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks says. The streamlined logo was part of the Southern comfort food restaurant's ongoing strategic transformation. Brooks poses the question of whether Cracker Barrel's choice not to go ahead with the logo change will have other ramifications for that transformation. Whatever it decides, the stakes are high, Brooks says: Cracker Barrel is spending $600 million to $700 million in capital expenditures on the transformation effort over three years. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1054 ET - Restaurant foot traffic appears to have tapered off in August compared with July, Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks says. Benchmark's panel of 31 restaurants had an average year-over-year traffic slow down of 175 basis points in August versus July, Brooks says. This trend is likely setting restaurant companies up for a tough 3Q season. About 40% of the restaurants in Benchmark's panel are on track to miss Wall Street estimates for same-store sales in the current quarter. Meanwhile, 13% of the restaurants are on track to beat with same-store sales by 150 basis points or more. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1029 ET - Tight credit spreads and strong performance in the European credit market have prompted issuance of asset-backed securities (ABS) in new jurisdictions such as Poland and Norway, S&P Global Ratings' Arnaud Checconi says in a report. Asset-backed securities are investments backed by income-producing assets such as loans and leases. "European ABS issuance volumes for 2025 are on track to exceed those in 2024," Checconi adds. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1022 ET - Investors are broadly predicting the death of software due to AI, but the industry's struggles will eventually subside, say JPMorgan analysts. The relative valuation of software against semiconductors is approaching a level that has historically yielded strong alpha for software stocks, the analysts say, adding that software stocks typically perform better in rate-cutting environments, which will likely take shape in coming weeks. The analysts also say HubSpot offered a strong response to the AI-induced-pressure narrative this week by saying "we provide the customer context to drive insights about growth--that is not there in the LLM." In addition, JPMorgan says HubSpot now offers a hybrid pricing model, rather than purely seat-based, offering a flexible way for customers to pay. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1020 ET - Airbus's commercial aircraft deliveries in August bring encouraging news for the European plane maker during a typically slow month, RBC Capital Markets analysts write in a research note. The group delivered 61 aircraft to customers last month, up 30% year-on-year. While the company's 434 deliveries so far this year are down 3% from the same period last year, analysts say the strong August figures suggest the group will be able to meet its 2025 target of about 820 aircraft deliveries. The fourth quarter has since 2010 accounted for nearly 33% of Airbus's annual deliveries, the analysts note. If the trend persists, they believe the company should be able to hit its delivery target. Airbus shares trade 1.5% lower at 184.08 euros. (william.gray@wsj.com)

1019 ET - Many long-time homeowners are sitting on substantial equity gains, but today's shifting market means selling successfully requires more strategy and preparation than in years past, Realtor.com says. Starting the process early--before the peak spring season--can make the difference between a smooth sale and missed opportunities. Nearly half--45.2%--of homeowners have lived in their homes for more than 15 years, with one in four staying put for more than 25 years. U.S. home prices have tripled from $145,000 in June 2000 to $435,300 in June 2025, according to the National Association of Realtors. For many owners, that means hundreds of thousands of dollars in potential equity. A typical homeowner who bought in 2005 has seen their home value rise by about 90%, boosting equity by more than $200,000. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1003 ET - Investors have expressed concern that Google AI Overviews will hurt HubSpot by taking away valuable web traffic. But the company anticipated this change years in advance and pivoted beyond search to alternate emerging channels including YouTube and podcasts, say JPMorgan analysts. HubSpot is now cited in more large language models than any other customer relationship management company and is now tracking when AI generates leads for customers' businesses, which will help the company sustain demand in the AI era. "HubSpot's adept maneuvering should be viewed as a feather in its cap and an ability to see around corners," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1237 ET - Strathcona Resources continues to buy up MEG Energy shares as it looks to position itself to oppose rival Cenovus' buyout of the Canadian oil sands producer. Strathcona said it had acquired another 6.03 million shares of MEG, which accepted a buyout by Cenovus last month. The new shares brings Strathcona's stake to 14.2% from 11.8%. Strathcona paid about C$172.7 for the latest batch. In May, Strathcona made an offer for MEG Energy, which the MEG board advised shareholders not to accept. MEG later accepted a C$7.9 billion buyout offer from Cenovus. Strathcona says it intends to vote its MEG shares against the resolution to approve the acquisition of MEG by Cenovus at a special meeting of MEG shareholders scheduled to be held on October 9, 2025. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0946 ET - Oil prices extend losses in afternoon trade, with Brent crude down 1.7% to $65.83 and WTI falling 1.9% to $62.28 a barrel. The benchmarks are on track for a weekly drop of around 2.5%, pressured by concerns over a looming supply glut as key OPEC+ members prepare to gather online Sunday and an unexpected rise in U.S. stockpiles last week. David Oxley, chief commodities economist at Capital Economics, says the firm expects OPEC+ to take a pause before raising output further from the second quarter of next year. Still, he noted that with prices having found some support over the summer, the group might feel it has room to bring more supply back to market sooner. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0934 ET - Crude futures are down for a third session and on track for weekly losses as the market watches for the weekend's OPEC+ meeting and digests the unexpected 2.4 million barrel build in U.S. crude stocks reported by the EIA. "Technical momentum appears to have completely reversed and is now heading lower," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. Increased OPEC+ barrels should keep the crude oil market well supplied, and the group could decide on bringing even more barrels online in October, he says. "With expectations of a surplus ahead, it is hard to get overly bullish."WTI is down 1.7% at $62.38 a barrel and Brent is off 1.6% at $65.93. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0924 ET - U.S. natural gas futures extend their slow grind higher, but with ample storage heading into the low-demand shoulder season keeping a limit on the rally. Focus remains on the production outlook as demand is likely to be a bit below normal in coming weeks, Gary Cunningham of Tradition Energy says in a note. Gas for January 2026 is testing resistance at $4.26, he adds, "but still has some upside potential as physical buyers who missed out on the retraction below $4 step into the market for their winter hedges." The Nymex front month is up 1% at $3.104/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0549 ET - The European natural-gas prices rise in early trade, as warm weather boosts demand for LNG and union strikes in France disrupt nuclear power generation. The benchmark Dutch TTF contract is up 0.8% to 32.69 euros a megawatt hour and on track for a weekly gain of 3.4%. High temperatures in parts of Japan have driven up electricity demand and led to fuel stockpile drawdowns, according to analysts at ANZ. Meanwhile, industrial action at France's top power producer, Electricite de France, curbed as much as 2 gigawatt of output. "France's power exports continued and operations at French LNG import terminals were not affected," the analysts say. "However, the market remains sensitive to any risks of supply disruptions or sudden jumps in demand." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0413 ET - Orsted's downgrade to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization is a small negative ahead of Friday's vote on a proposed 60 billion Danish kroner rights issue, Jefferies analyst Ahmed Farman writes. The company lowered its full-year Ebitda guidance to between 24 billion and 27 billion kroner, from 25 billion-28 billion kroner previously, driven by lower-than-normal offshore wind speeds during July and August. Orsted also mentioned it expects a construction delay at its Greater Changhua 2b project, which will also adversely affect Ebitda in 2025. Jefferies expects details on the rights issue in the coming days. Shares rise 0.9%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0402 ET - Oil prices are set for a weekly loss of more than 1% for the first time in three weeks amid concerns over OPEC+ supply and an unexpected build in U.S. crude stockpiles. In early trade, Brent crude is down 0.5% to $66.67 a barrel, while WTI falls 0.5% to $63.13 a barrel. OPEC+'s online meeting on Sunday will be closely watched by traders, as further production increases would exacerbate concerns over a looming supply glut. Meanwhile, a bearish U.S. inventory report further weighed on sentiment. The latest EIA data showed U.S. crude stocks rose by 2.4 million barrels last week--against expectations of a 2-million-barrel fall--and are now at their highest level since early August, according to ANZ. Further price declines are though capped by risks of disruption to Russian supplies from Ukrainian attacks on key energy infrastructure and the spectrum of tougher U.S. sanctions. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0308 ET - Orsted's guidance downgrade is a negative ahead of the rights issue prospectus which is expected early next week, RBC analyst Alexander Wheeler writes. Following lower-than-normal offshore wind speeds, Orsted lowered its 2025 Ebitda guidance to 24 billion-27 billion Danish kroner ($3.7 billon to $4.2 billion), from 25 billion-28 billion kroner previously. Company consensus is at 26.5 billion kroner, Wheeler adds. Lower wind speeds have impacted Ebitda by 1.2 billion kroner, with a construction delay at the Greater Changhua 2b project providing a 300 million kroner hit. Additionally, commissioning of Greater Changhua 2b has been delayed from late 2025 to the third quarter of 2026 due to damage to an export cable, he adds. Shares fall 1.7%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: S.Africa's electricity regulator backs ArcelorMittal's bid for discounted tariffs
data.description:
Key points:
  • Regulator says it does not dictate electricity charges
  • Steelmaker must re-apply for favourable terms with Eskom
  • ArcelorMittal closing down plants partly due to high power costs

ArcelorMittal South Africa meets the criteria for discounted electricity tariffs in terms of set rules, the country's energy regulator said on Friday, backing a plea by the loss-making steelmaker.

The regulator, however, said it did not have the authority to dictate an electricity tariff and ArcelorMittal would have to negotiate favourable terms with state-owned power utility Eskom.

ArcelorMittal is winding down its money-losing South African long steel operations, which have been weighed down by high costs, including electricity expenses, while battling weak demand and an influx of imports.

The steelmaker petitioned the National Energy Regulator of South Africa (NERSA) to review a decision by Eskom to reject its application for discounted tariffs.

Eskom argued that ArcelorMittal did not meet the criteria for negotiated pricing agreements (NPAs), which are typically offered to energy-intensive industrial power users.

"NERSA considered the eligibility criteria and other factors

outlined in the NPA framework and concluded that ArcelorMittal South Africa substantially complied with the prescribed

criteria," the regulator said in a statement.

ArcelorMittal said this week it was winding down its long steel plants at Newcastle and Vereeniging as planned on September 30, after failing to secure government concessions on utility charges and import duties, among other demands.

The closure of the plants could result in the loss of more than 3,500 direct jobs, the company said.

ArcelorMittal said the state-owned Industrial Development Corporation, an 8% shareholder in ArcelorMittal South Africa, was conducting due diligence on the steelmaker.

data.title: SuperRare to Launch “Facade” by Gavin Meeler on September 10
data.description:
SuperRare is set to feature “Facade”, a new collection by artist Gavin Meeler, starting September 10. The drop includes three unique 1/1 pieces—On the Brink, Unraveled, and The Glare—each with a reserve price of 1.5 ETH. The expressive, abstract works will be available on the on-chain art marketplace for a limited bidding period.

RARE Info

SuperRare is a platform designed for the issuance, collection, and trading of unique digital art supported by non-fungible tokens (NFTs) on the Ethereum blockchain.

On SuperRare, artists can create their artworks directly from their own wallets, and every bid, sale, and collector is recorded and stored in the token's history on the blockchain. Using to Ethereum, the platform ensures the security and verification of authorship and ownership of the art.

SuperRare provides an opportunity for artists to sell their works directly to collectors, and collectors can acquire unique and exclusive digital art pieces. This opens up new possibilities for artists and collectors in the digital world.
